Traditional security measures often rely on reactive approaches, addressing threats only after they materialise. However, strategic foresight offers a proactive methodology, enabling institutions to anticipate potential security challenges and develop long-term, resilient strategies. Moreover, the use of strategic foresight enhances interdisciplinary cooperation among security experts, policymakers, and academic researchers. This collaboration helps develop holistic security protocols that balance innovation with risk mitigation. Institutions that implement foresight-driven research security mechanisms can create more adaptive policies, ensuring their research enterprises remain safeguarded against unforeseen disruptions. This chapter explores the application of strategic foresight to assess potential future risks to research security frameworks. It examines how universities can use foresight methodologies – such as scenario planning, horizon scanning, and trend analysis – to identify emerging threats and vulnerabilities before they compromise institutional integrity. The application of strategic foresight also encourages a culture of preparedness, allowing universities to assess risks stemming from technological advancements, geopolitical shifts, and evolving regulatory environments. By adopting forward-looking strategies, institutions can mitigate security risks without stifling academic freedom or international collaboration.
Keywords: strategic foresight; research security; cyber threats; risk mitigation; interdisciplinary cooperation

title: Editorial: ENPER2024 - three decades of endomembrane research
authors: Gian Pietro Di Sansebastiano; Cláudia Pereira
Tags: plant research; membrane biology; collaboration; trafficking mechanisms; ENPER2024
abstract: Since its establishment in 1996, the European Network for Plant Endomembrane Research (ENPER) has provided a unique forum for researchers working on plant membrane biology. The ENPER meetings have played an important role in fostering collaboration and discussion across disciplines, bringing together scientists studying vesicle trafficking, organelle biogenesis, membrane signalling, and plant-microbe interactions. As one of the longest-running conferences dedicated to plant membrane biology, ENPER provides a platform where researchers -from early-career scientists to established researchers -share new discoveries and emerging concepts in endomembrane trafficking with a collaborative approach that contributed to accelerate progresses in the past decades. The ENPER2024 meeting, held in Porto, Portugal, continued this tradition by bringing together researchers working on diverse aspects of membrane dynamics, protein transport, and organelle communication.This Research Topic, ENPER2024 -Three Decades of Endomembrane Research, compiles contributions that reflect both the diversity of approaches and the continuing evolution of the field. The articles collected here highlight recent advances in understanding trafficking mechanisms, vacuolar and endosomal functions, and the interplay between membrane systems and plant signalling pathways.Different contributions explore the molecular machinery governing vesicle trafficking and membrane organization with an impact of cell biology of all eukaryotes and not only plants. In the review "The clues offered by SNAREs on the vacuoles of plants and animals" (https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/plant-science/articles/10.3389/fpls.2025.1599323/full), Barozzi and colleagues examine the conserved and divergent roles of SNARE proteins in mediating membrane fusion events. By comparing plant and animal vacuolar systems, the authors emphasize how studies in different organisms can illuminate the evolutionary conservation of trafficking mechanisms while also revealing plant-specific adaptations. Their synthesis highlights the importance of SNARE complexes in maintaining vacuolar identity and regulating membrane fusion processes essential for cellular homeostasis. The importance of trafficking components in plant development is further illustrated by the original research article "Exocyst subunits EXO70B1 and B2 contribute to stomatal dynamics and cell wall modifications" (https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/plant-science/articles/10.3389/fpls.2025.1694769/full). Drs and co-workers investigate the roles of specific exocyst subunits in stomatal function, providing evidence that EXO70B1 and EXO70B2 contribute to the regulation of cell wall remodelling and guard cell dynamics. These findings underscore the complex roles of the exocyst complex in coordinating targeted secretion and cellular morphogenesis, particularly in specialized cell types such as guard cells.Membrane dynamics are also central to organelle biogenesis and differentiation. In the study "The fate and function of the Arabidopsis Class I formin AtFH1 during central vacuole biogenesis in the rhizodermis" (https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/plant-science/articles/10.3389/fpls.2025.1685260/full), Kočová and colleagues explore how the actinassociated protein AtFH1 contributes to vacuole formation. Their work reveals a dynamic interplay between cytoskeletal elements and membrane remodelling processes during the development of the central vacuole, an organelle essential for plant cell expansion and physiological regulation.Beyond developmental processes, the endomembrane system also plays a critical role in plant responses to environmental cues and biotic interactions. The mini-review "Salicylic acid: new pathways arising?" (https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/plant-science/articles/10.3389/fpls.2025.1681791/full) by Müller and Scheuring revisits the emerging connections between salicylic acid signalling and membrane trafficking pathways. The authors discuss recent evidence suggesting that hormone signalling and endomembrane dynamics are closely connected, opening new perspectives on how intracellular transport processes influence immune responses and stress signalling. In the mini-review "Pathogen effector tactics to suppress plant endomembrane system" (https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/plant-science/articles/10.3389/fpls.2026.1776548/abstract), Eunssok Park and her team summarize current knowledge on how pathogen effectors target host trafficking pathways to promote infection. By interfering with vesicle trafficking and membrane identity, pathogens can disrupt host immune responses and cellular organization. This review highlights the endomembrane system as both a critical component of plant defence and a vulnerable target exploited by pathogens.Taken together, the contributions assembled in this Research Topic reflect the broad but central role of membrane trafficking in plant cell biology. From vesicle fusion machinery and cytoskeleton-membrane interactions to hormone signalling and pathogen interference, the endomembrane system emerges as a key integrator of cellular processes. For a long period in which research was focused on basic traffic mechanisms and complexity of the plant cell had to be fully recognized, we assist now to the emerging of the intricate relation between endomembranes and cellular responses to all possible external stimuli. The studies published in this collection as the research conducted in the ENPER community in general, also illustrate how combining different methodological approaches, including molecular genetics, cell biology, and comparative analysis, continues to advance our understanding of intracellular transport mechanisms. Importantly, the collection reflects the collaborative and interdisciplinary spirit that has characterized the ENPER community for nearly three decades. The ENPER meetings have consistently fostered interactions between laboratories working on complementary aspects of membrane biology, facilitating the exchange of ideas and the development of new conceptual frameworks. As new technologies -such as advanced live-cell imaging, quantitative proteomics, and computational modelling -become increasingly accessible, the field is well positioned to address longstanding questions regarding membrane organization and dynamics.Looking forward, several challenges remain. Understanding how trafficking pathways are coordinated at the systems level, how membrane identity is established and maintained, and how organelles communicate during development and stress responses are among the key questions that will shape future research. Addressing these questions will require continued collaboration across disciplines and the integration of experimental and theoretical approaches.The contributions gathered in ENPER2024 -Three Decades of Endomembrane Research provide valuable insights into these topics and highlight the vitality of the plant endomembrane research community. We thank all authors, reviewers, and editors who contributed to this Research Topic and hope that the work presented here will stimulate further research and collaboration within this dynamic field.
Keywords: endomembrane; plant biology; vesicle trafficking; membrane dynamics; SNARE proteins; exocyst complex; pathogen interaction

title: Hidden Harm—Exploring the Utility of Geostatistical Analysis to Identify Child Criminal Exploitation (CCE)
authors: Antoinette Keaney-Bell; Colm Walsh
OSINT_Source_Types: Public Records; Government Data; News Media
Tags: Geostatistical Techniques; Preventive Strategies; Resource Allocation; Spatial Analysis; Interdisciplinary Study
abstract: This interdisciplinary study integrates criminological theory with geospatial methods to analyse large, multi-format datasets using geostatistical techniques. The aim is to predict where Child Criminal Exploitation (CCE) is likely to cluster, based on the spatial convergence of contextual risk factors. Drawing on insights from General Strain Theory (GST) and prior research on CCE, this study integrated seven open-source datasets capturing educational attainment, age demographics, violent crime, deprivation, and paramilitary-related violence. These variables were operationalised to construct a proxy measure for strain. Spatial analysis was conducted using ArcGIS Pro, including the Data Interoperability extension, to enable efficient integration and interrogation of multi-format geospatial data. Geospatial analysis demonstrated that contextual risk factors for CCE are spatially clustered. Using four search parameters, a small subset of wards with elevated risk were identified. This resulted in a reduction in ward locations by 85–99%, land area under investigation from 14.45% to 0.84%, and affected population from 17.91% to 1.41%, enabling more targeted and efficient resource allocation. As understanding of the contextual factors contributing to CCE improves, this methodological approach offers scalable and data-driven means of identifying high-risk areas. By integrating geospatial analysis with criminological theory, the model supports more effective safeguarding strategies and prioritisation of limited public resources. This study is limited by the absence of multi-agency datasets, which were beyond its scope. Future research aims to incorporate cross-sector data to validate and refine the model through ground-truthing, enhancing its predictive accuracy and practical applicability.
Keywords: Child Criminal Exploitation; Geospatial Analysis; Risk Factors; Criminology; Data Integration

title: 00 | INTEGRATED STRATEGIES FOR PLASTIC MANAGEMENT: MONITORING, BIOLOGICAL IMPACT, AND CIRCULAR PERSPECTIVES ROUND TABLE REPORT – III PLASTAMINATION CONGRESS - 13 FEBRUARY 2026
authors: Società Italiana di Biologia Sperimentale
venue: Journal of Biological Research - Bollettino della Società Italiana di Biologia Sperimentale
OSINT_Source_Types: Social Media; Public Records; News Media
Tags: Sustainability; Plastic Management; One Health; Marine Strategy; Environmental Education
abstract: Introduction The PLASTAMINATION Congress held on 13 February 2026 at the University of Palermo, convened a multidisciplinary panel of experts to address the escalating challenge of micro- and nanoplastic (MNP) contamination. Characterized during the session as a silent invasion MNP pollution represents a systemic threat that bridges environmental degradation and human pathology. This round table synthesized perspectives on the entire plastic lifecycle: from urban point sources and regional monitoring to ecotoxicological risks and advanced materials engineering. The discussion underscored that the traditional conceptual boundary between the environment and the human organism is no longer sustainable, necessitating a coordinated scientific and institutional response grounded in the One Health framework. Science communication and public responsibility The round table opened with a reflection on the role of science communication and public responsibility, moderated by Giorgia Görner Enrile, journalist for SciliaMedica.it and ilSicilia.it, who framed the discussion around the relationship between scientific knowledge, information, and society. Communicating health today means more than sharing scientific results, because communication itself carries an ethical responsibility toward society. The III PLASTAMINATION Conference 2026 and the project PLASTAMINATION Poly (Lactic Acid) plastics contamination therefore address not only the issue of plastic and MNP contamination, but also the way scientific evidence reaches the public and the effects it may generate in terms of trust, fear, or awareness. The One Health perspective reminds us that environmental, animal, and human health remain deeply interconnected, but it also highlights the responsibility involved in communicating these topics, since scientific data and research findings can easily enter public debate and sometimes become instrumentalized within environmental, social, or political narratives. Public discussion does not necessarily represent a problem, but difficulties arise when the complexity of scientific research becomes reduced to simplified messages or slogans. This dynamic becomes even more evident within the contemporary information ecosystem, where media attention often focuses on headlines rather than on scientific context, while information increasingly circulates through social networks whose visibility mechanisms depend more on algorithms than on the reliability of sources. In this environment, communication about health and environmental risks should not only inform but also encourage critical thinking, helping people recognize reliable information and distinguish between evidence, interpretation, and sensationalism. Transparency therefore plays a crucial role in scientific and institutional communication, but transparency does not mean oversimplification or reassurance at any cost, since trust grows from clarity and from the recognition of the limits that accompany scientific knowledge. Environmental monitoring and infrastructure vulnerabilities Effective mitigation of marine plastic pollution begins with robust environmental surveillance and standardized monitoring programmes. Dr. Salvatore Campanella and Dr. Alessandro Aglialoro, ARPA Sicilia, presented the environmental monitoring activities carried out by the regional agency within the framework of the Marine Strategy Framework Directive (MSFD, Directive 2008/56/EC). This European policy instrument aims to achieve and maintain the Good Environmental Status (GES) of marine waters through coordinated monitoring systems implemented by Member States and supported in Italy by the Ministry for the Environment, ISPRA, and the National System for Environmental Protection (SNPA). Within this framework, ARPA Sicilia conducts periodic monitoring campaigns in coastal and offshore waters using standardized sampling protocols. These surveys include the collection and analysis of seawater, marine sediments, and biological matrices (biota) in order to assess environmental quality and detect the presence of anthropogenic contaminants. Monitoring activities also address the distribution of marine litter and microplastics, including the assessment of floating debris, beached litter, and microplastic particles in the water column and sediments, as required by the indicators of Descriptor 10 (Marine Litter) of the Marine Strategy. The data generated through these monitoring programmes contribute to national and European environmental reporting systems and support the scientific assessment of the ecological status of the Mediterranean Sea. These datasets provide an essential basis for environmental policy, risk assessment, and the development of mitigation strategies at both national and European levels. In addition to monitoring activities, ARPA Sicilia participates in international scientific cooperation initiatives and promotes environmental education and awareness programmes, including outreach activities aimed at schools and local communities, with the objective of increasing public understanding of marine pollution and fostering sustainable environmental practices. However, the urban origin of plastic contaminants remains a critical factor in their transfer to aquatic environments. Prof. Michele Torregrossa (University of Palermo) highlighted the dual role of wastewater treatment plants (WWTPs) in the environmental dynamics of microplastic pollution. On the one hand, conventional wastewater treatment processes, including primary sedimentation, biological treatment, and secondary clarification, together with advanced technologies such as membrane bioreactors (MBRs), are capable of removing a substantial fraction of microplastics from treated effluents. Several studies report removal efficiencies frequently exceeding 90% for larger microplastic particles, although efficiency depends on particle size and plant configuration. On the other hand, WWTPs act as accumulation points for microplastics, since most of the particles retained during treatment are transferred to sewage sludge. This creates a secondary environmental concern, particularly when sludge is reused in agriculture or disposed of in landfills, potentially reintroducing microplastics into terrestrial ecosystems. The situation may be further exacerbated by extreme weather events associated with climate change. During intense rainfall, combined sewer systems may generate Combined Sewer Overflows (CSOs), temporarily bypassing wastewater treatment plants and discharging untreated wastewater directly into receiving water bodies. Under these conditions, several studies have reported that microplastic concentrations in urban runoff and CSO discharges can be up to 5 or 10 times higher than those measured in treated wastewater effluents. This increase is mainly related to the wash-off of urban surfaces, the mobilization of plastic particles accumulated within sewer networks, and the resuspension of sediments during high-flow conditions. These findings highlight the need for integrated management strategies addressing both urban wastewater infrastructure and plastic pollution mitigation, in order to reduce the transfer of microplastics from urban systems to aquatic environments. Biological risks From Ecotoxicology to Human Health The transition of plastics from environmental matrices to biological tissues was a central theme. Prof. Federica Scalia (UniKore) presented evidence regarding the invisible risks of biodegradable polymers, specifically Polylactic Acid (PLA). Utilizing the zebrafish model, her research demonstrated that PLA nanoplastics can bioaccumulate in zebrafish, especially in the gastrointestinal system, within 120 hours post-fertilization. These exposures were linked to significant gene expression alterations, specifically in cellular stress markers, suggesting that eco-friendly alternatives require rigorous toxicological scrutiny during embryonic development. These findings resonate with the human health perspectives shared by Prof. Stefania D’Angelo (Parthenope University of Naples). MNPs have shifted from being external contaminants to internal systemic constituents, detected in the lungs, liver, placenta, and brain. The discussion detailed how MNPs trigger oxidative stress, chronic inflammation, and the disruption of critical biological barriers. Of particular clinical concern is the presence of MNPs within atherosclerotic plaques, which is increasingly associated with a higher risk of major cardiovascular events, and their potential role in neuroinflammatory processes related to dementia. Circular solutions and materials science To counter these threats, the panel explored resilient technological pathways. Prof. Annamaria Visco (University of Messina), principal investigator of the European LIFE RESTART project, presented an applied model of circular economy in the field of polymeric materials, based on the valorization of agro-industrial by-products and their integration into biodegradable polymer matrices. The project, funded under the LIFE Programme of the European Union, aims to develop sustainable composite materials obtained from residual biomass derived from agricultural and agro-industrial supply chains, contributing to the reduction of fossil-based raw materials and promoting new circular production models. The research activities carried out by the University of Messina research group have led to the development of polymeric biocomposites obtained by incorporating plant residues and agro-industrial by-products into biodegradable matrices. This approach allows the valorization of organic waste streams, transforming them into high-value materials, while simultaneously reducing the environmental impact associated with conventional plastic production. A key element of the project is the establishment of the “Fabbrica Zero” pilot plant in Roccavaldina (Messina), a demonstration facility designed to transfer laboratory research results to an industrial scale. Within this framework, materials developed in academic laboratories are processed through technologies compatible with existing industrial transformation processes, demonstrating the technical feasibility of producing biocomposites from residual biomass. During the discussion, the economic dimension also clearly emerged as a critical factor influencing the large-scale adoption of these materials. The development and production costs of sustainable biocomposites are currently higher than those of conventional fossil-based plastics, particularly during the early phases of technological deployment. As a result, many companies face significant challenges in independently sustaining the transition toward more sustainable materials. It was therefore highlighted that a structural transformation of production systems requires not only scientific innovation but also targeted policy measures and economic incentives capable of supporting companies in adopting environmentally sustainable materials and processes. In this context, Prof. Andrea Pace (University of Palermo) emphasized that the development of new materials must be framed within a broader perspective integrating scientific research, industrial policies, and innovation governance. According to Pace, the transition toward more sustainable polymeric materials cannot rely solely on technological progress but requires the creation of innovation ecosystems in which universities, research centers, industries, and public institutions collaborate in a coordinated and structured manner. It emerged that strengthening technology transfer mechanisms, supporting applied research, and developing dedicated funding instruments could play a crucial role in accelerating the integration of sustainable materials into industrial systems. In this perspective, policy tools such as incentives for companies adopting low-impact materials, green public procurement strategies, and innovation programmes promoted at the European level could significantly reduce the economic barriers that still limit the widespread adoption of these technologies. Finally, Dr. Dario Scalia, Plastic Free Onlus highlighted the crucial role of civil society in addressing plastic pollution. Alongside technological solutions and industrial policies, environmental volunteering initiatives, awareness campaigns, and territorial clean-up activities represent concrete tools to reduce plastic dispersion in natural ecosystems and to promote collective responsibility in environmental protection. During the discussion, the importance of environmental education targeting younger generations was also strongly emphasized. Educational programmes in schools, awareness initiatives, and participatory activities involving students represent key instruments for fostering a culture of sustainability from an early age. It emerged that engaging children and young people in environmental education initiatives can play a fundamental role in shaping long-term behavioral change and in promoting responsible attitudes toward environmental protection. Conclusion The PLASTAMINATION round table concluded that the fight against plastic contamination requires an integrated, multi-scalar approach. Enhancing the filtration capacity of WWTPs is insufficient if the resulting sludge is not managed or if materials are not designed for sustainability from their inception. The detection of epigenetic impacts and the systemic accumulation of plastics in vital human organs mandate a paradigm shift: plastic is no longer merely a waste management issue but a critical determinant of global public health.
Keywords: Microplastics; Environmental Monitoring; Public Health; Circular Economy; Plastic Pollution

title: Building a Digital Media Platform for the European Public Sphere
authors: Christoph Neuberger
OSINT_Source_Types: Social Media; News Media; Public Records
Tags: digital sovereignty; public value; journalistic standards; platform design; democracy
abstract: Introduction The currently dominant digital platforms are associated with many negative effects on democratic societies, such as disinformation, hate speech, and propaganda. In the case of X, the platform has become a personal megaphone for its owner Elon Musk. The “Great Sysop” has gained considerable power over public opinion formation and is promoting “platform illiberalism” (Magalhães et al.). However, Twitter has also added valuable features that have enhanced public communication, particulary in journalism, which is the focus of the present article. Journalists used Twitter for purposes such as live reporting, news aggregation, including citizens as sources, organising debates (Artwick), and fact checking (Coddington et al.). Hermida coined the term “ambient journalism” to describe journalism’s role on Twitter as a novel “awareness system”. In 2014, a newsroom survey in Germany compared the use of different platforms in journalism (Neuberger et al.). Twitter was most often used for brief breaking news and real-time reporting. Editorial staff also utilised Twitter and Facebook relatively often for investigation and monitoring. Twitter was used especially to contact elite sources, such as experts and prominent people, and for monitoring their public appearances. Twitter and Facebook were the preferred platforms for user participation in journalism. Twitter served as a testing ground for new forms of journalism. But this is all history. These achievements have been lost since Elon Musk took over the platform. This raises the questions: how should a common good-oriented platform be designed and governed? What lessons can be learned from the journalistic adaptation of Twitter? I will address these questions by focussing on the transnational European public sphere. European Public Sphere as Goal The philosopher Jürgen Habermas (Für Europa), who passed away in March 2026, continued to advocate for European autonomy, integration, and democratisation, particularly in the context of today's polycrisis. He believed that establishing a common European public sphere by transnationalising the many national public spheres is essential (Habermas, Demokratie 188–91). But the European public sphere still has significant shortcomings (e.g., Brüggemann and Kleinen-von Königslöw; Brüggemann et al.). There is a lack of European media, audiences interested in European issues, and European perspectives in national media. Europeanisation is particularly evident in the vertical dimension (“looking towards Brussels”), but less so in the horizontal dimension. Looking beyond national borders at other national discourses is therefore crucial here. Another weakness is the low citizen participation in the European public sphere. The question of a European public sphere has regained importance in recent years. In light of the current geopolitical situation, the European Union (EU) is striving for greater digital sovereignty. This concept emphasises state authority over the Internet, replacing the earlier concept of “multistakeholderism”, which viewed the Internet as a distinct, decentralised, and transnational space beyond the reach of politics (Pohle and Santianello). The call for digital sovereignty encompasses not only the technical and economic dimension, but also the political and cultural dimension. Digital sovereignty “must not be conflated with isolation or protectionism, in the European context, it can be described as the capacity of being independent while remaining open to collaboration and committed to shared values such as transparency, democracy, and the rule of law” (Bria et al. 10). European values are often cited as the goal of digital sovereignty (e.g., Di Marco et al.; Kagermann and Wilhelm; Roberts et al.), but are not precisely defined. The EU is often criticised for over-regulating platforms and AI, hindering innovation and competitiveness. However, there are serious doubts as to whether this criticism is valid (Bradford, False Choice). Nevertheless, the EU must not only regulate, but also provide better platforms. This requires a change in mindset: regulation only defines the minimum normative requirements that must be met in every case. This negative approach must then be complemented by a positive one that describes how platforms can promote the common good. The EU could offer a more appealing alternative to the libertarian approach of the US and the authoritarian approach of China (Bradford, Digital Empires). Public Sphere and Public Value But how can the common good be defined? In the media sector, it is usually referred to as “public value”. This management concept was introduced by Mark H. Moore in his seminal book Creating Public Value. It was intended as a practical framework for public sector management, offering an alternative to the market-oriented, neo-liberal “new public management” and its premise that the public good is simply the sum of individual consumer interests. In contrast to this market-oriented view, the public value concept focusses on contributions to society’s common good beyond individual self-interest (Alford and Hughes 132; Moore, “Reflections” 366–9). The BBC adopted it in its programmatic statement Building Public Value. In this statement, public value was defined through three “public principles” (universality, fairness and equity, accountability) and five “public purposes” (democratic value, cultural and creative value, educational value, social and community value, global value) (7–8). A recent paper, Creating and Measuring Dynamic Public Value at the BBC, distinguishes between individual value, social value, and industrial value (Mazzucato et al. 23–8). The BBC’s public value test for public broadcasting outlets has been adopted by many EU countries (Collins; Donders). Some scholars have criticised a lack of clarity about what public value means substantially (e.g., Benington 42–6; Collins 175). Others have argued that public value cannot be defined “in some absolute sense” (Alford and Hughes 133) because public value must be, at least partially, open to the outcomes of democratic political processes (Alford and O’Flynn 175–8; Benington 233–4). The concept of hybrid common good offers a solution for these contradictory expectations (Blum; Neuberger, Auftrag). According to this approach, the common good comprises a substantive component consisting of liberal-democratic values to be realised in the value creation process, alongside public discourse as procedural component, in which those values are interpreted and concretised. These values are the “guardrails”, setting the boundaries for possible outcomes of the democratic public discourse, and also the standards for the discourse itself. How can the European values be defined more precisely? One obvious option is to derive them from normative theories of democracy. Several proposals have been made as to how normative theories of democracy can be applied to the public sphere and journalism (e.g., Christians et al. 95–105, 125–7; Ferree et al.; Gerhards; Martinsen; McQuail; Strömbäck). While these typologies differ, the theories agree that the media should at least create a common public sphere and a shared body of knowledge on current topics of public interest. Both are necessary prerequisites for the formation of individual and collective opinion. Valid and shared knowledge requires news production to be based on journalistic standards and to allow freedom of information and equal access to knowledge. However, beyond this, the theories make different demands regarding the process of opinion formation. Deliberative theory sets higher normative expectations for discourse progression than liberal and participatory theory (Martinsen). It demands a rational, respectful, and domination-free discourse in which the persuasiveness of the better argument—in the best case—leads to consensus. In summary, nine values of the public sphere can be derived from theories of democracy, whose normative expectations partially overlap: news and discourse quality, freedom, equality, diversity, power symmetry, criticism and control, security, and integration (Neuberger, Öffentlichkeit). Initiatives to Build a European Media Platform A more democratic digital public sphere cannot be achieved simply by regulating existing platform and AI companies. It is equally important to create media platforms and AI applications that are oriented towards the common good as an alternative to the commercial offerings that currently dominate the market. Although Habermas acknowledged the potential of the digital public sphere to contribute to the emancipation of citizens, he viewed the Internet as an “ambivalent explosive force” (Habermas, New Structural 35). He missed a “substitute for the professional selection and discursive examination of contents based on accepted cognitive standards” (38–39) on these platforms. Since 2017, there has been an ongoing discussion about the idea of a European media platform oriented towards the common good that would fulfil European values, create a European public sphere, and strengthen Europe’s digital sovereignty. Ulrich Wilhelm, the director of the Bavarian Broadcasting Corporation (Bayerischer Rundfunk), was the first and strongest advocate of this idea. He expressed concern that “Europe is in danger of losing digital sovereignty over its defining values” (Wilhelm 15; translated by the author). Van Dijck, Poell, and de Waal (146) outlined a “value-centric design of the platform society”, which should realise “public values” such as safety, privacy, transparency, accuracy, democratic control, quality, accessibility, comprehensiveness, equality, inclusiveness, fair treatment, responsibility, and accountability (139–41). Hillje (133–60) proposed four elements for a “Platform Europe”: a European newsroom and a European news service, opportunities for citizens to participate, a cultural connection representing Europe's diversity and common “European Way of Life”, and apps for citizens to take action and interact. Another plan proposed the creation of a European infrastructure for public service media and other media, as well as for science, culture, and citizens (Kagermann and Wilhelm). What has been achieved until now? Not much. Several proposals have been made to create a European media platform. The Council for European Public Space, an NGO, has developed a draft for a decentralised platform for trustworthy news called “Delphi” (Pfeffer). The Public Spaces Incubator (PSI) brings together public service media from different countries to work together to develop platforms for better public dialogue. The PSI toolkit will be made publicly available as open-source software by 2027. The European Cultural Foundation develops and supports cultural initiatives that foster “belonging, solidarity, and democratic values—building connections across Europe and empowering communities to shape a shared future.” It presented the draft for European Digital Public Spaces back in 2020. France and Germany intend to expand their joint cultural television channel arte into a European media platform (Der Beauftragte der Bundesregierung für Kultur und Medien). In 2025, the Second German Television (ZDF) presented its plan for a public-interest-oriented Digital Public Open Space (Dogruel et al.). There are many plans, but it remains to be seen whether they can be successfully realised in future. The drafts for a European media platform largely leave open the question of which values they intend to achieve and how. Only smaller projects promoting European news exchange and discourse have been established so far. One such project is the press review euro|topics – European Press Roundup, which monitors European discourses and translates the main contributions from leading newspapers into five languages. This project was founded by the German Federal Agency for Civic Education (Bundeszentrale für politische Bildung, bpb) as early as 2005. A European Perspective (AEP) is a transnational European newsroom, launched by public service media in 2021. Its 23 member organisations from 17 countries exchange news and translate news using the AI-assisted EuroVox tool. In 2024, AEP launched YEP News (Your European Perspective), a multilingual news aggregator that reports on major European and global issues. Outline for a Common Good-Oriented European Platform for Journalism A compelling concept for such a platform is still lacking. While Twitter certainly opened up new possibilities for journalism, it was not primarily created to improve democracy. Therefore, simply copying it would not suffice. It would be better to first define the modules required for a European media platform that is oriented towards the common good. In line with the mission of public service media, such a platform could encompass journalism, education, advice, culture, and entertainment. This article, however, focusses on journalism, which contributes to the common good in two ways: by producing and disseminating news and comments, and by moderating public discourse. Unlike traditional mass media, digital platforms are not limited to the linear dissemination of news and comments. They also enable interactive communication, broad participation in the public discourse, and the archiving of verified news and justified opinions. Taking these digital capabilities into account, a matrix of journalistic platform modules can be developed that integrates dissemination, discourse, and archiving (table 1). These modules complement each other and are closely interconnected: news and comments distributed by the media provide ongoing opportunities for public discourse, during which their justification is questioned and clarified through argumentation. Validated news and opinions, along with their justifications, are stored in the archive and can be reintroduced into the process, serving as context for current events or providing argumentative support. However, they can also be corrected and supplemented themselves. Knowledge Assessment Journalistic intermediation Short-term: Production and dissemination News Comment Professional production and linear dissemination Medium-term: Public discourse Epistemic validation Opinion formation process Professional moderation (ensuring broad participation and deliberative quality of discourses) Long-term: Archiving Validated knowledge as context Documentation of opinion and its justification (pros and cons) Professional archiving and referencing Table 1: Modules of a journalistic platform The envisioned journalistic platform modules should connect three spatial levels: the local, national, and European. Yochai Benkler’s normative concept of an integrated networked public sphere, which he outlined back in 2006, is helpful here. The goal of this concept is to strike the right balance between openness and closedness in public discourse. Both are necessary, but also risky: openness brings participation and diversity, but also information overload, loss of news quality (disinformation), and rude discourse (hate speech). On the side of closedness, gatekeeping ensures news and discourse quality, but focussing attention on a small number of relevant topics (agenda-setting) can lead to a concentration of power and lack of diversity. According to Benkler, the tension between openness and closedness should be resolved by distributing specific tasks across the different levels of the public sphere and by the gradual processing of news and comments in different phases. This begins with a high degree of diversity in topics and related news and comments across numerous mini-publics at the local level, with strong citizen participation. This continues at the national and European level, mediated by journalistic selection and moderation. At the European level, exchanges between the national publics should be encouraged. At higher levels and in later phases, the publics merge and diversity in rational discourse is reduced when selection decisions are made about the relevance of topics, the accuracy of news, and the (better) justification of opinions. This narrowing of the focus in a bottom-up process should not be centralised or finalised. Rather than the “hard” selection decisions (gatekeeping) of traditional mass media, a recommendation-based approach and “soft” (revisable) selection process (gatewatching: see Bruns) should be adopted. This multi-level architecture helps to ensure that the concrete local experiences of citizens remain connected to the political decisions made at higher levels in distant capitals. Topics can be covered and discussed on platforms in the long term, with knowledge and opinions from the archive providing context. Of course, theories of democracy should not be the only normative benchmark for the European media platform. Additional modules are required to strengthen identity, community building, social cohesion, and connections between different lifeworld experiences (Bengesser). Steps Towards a Common Good-Oriented European Media Platform What steps are necessary for the design of such a European media platform? Digital transformation is an ongoing process of change, meaning that the conditions for realising democratic values are also perpetually changing (Coeckelbergh, Digital Humanism 3). This requires continuous learning and weighing up of new chances and risks. Appropriate learning environments include innovation labs (García-Avilés) and pioneer communities (Hepp and Loosen). This process of experimentation could be observed in the case of Twitter and is also currently evident in the field of generative artificial intelligence (Jungherr). Negative effects have become apparent, such as incorrect outputs, bias, unfairness, opacity, copyright violations, and environmental costs. Conversely, visions were developed on how to strengthen democracy with AI and how to democratise AI (Coeckelbergh, Undermines 70-119). There are already many AI applications that are conducive to democracy: LLM-powered chatbots can enhance news diversity (Heitz et al.), improve the quality of political discourse (Argyle et al.), and verify political information (Kuznetsova et al.). Pioneer communities such as the German AI for Media Network support systematic learning. An experimental approach is needed to exploit the opportunities and limits of digital technologies for democracy, following the concepts of “experimental democracy” by John Dewey and “piecemeal technology” by Karl R. Popper. Both authors recommended taking small, reversible steps to test new paths in democracy. In addition to the editorial development of a European media platform outlined here, the necessary institutional and legal frameworks must be created. If a new organisation is not established, existing public service media organisations should cooperate within the framework of the European Broadcasting Union (EBU), for example. Furthermore, a political consensus must be reached among EU member states. This will certainly be the greatest challenge. The rise of right-wing populist parties and the erosion of democracy in several EU countries is both the main reason for taking measures to strengthen the European public sphere, but also the biggest obstacle. Given this challenging situation, the only feasible approach is a gradual rollout of a European media platform, starting with a small number of countries and focussing on verified news as minimum consensus. The hope must be that the creation of a European public sphere will raise democratic awareness and promote European solidarity. Digital platforms can be useful in this respect; Twitter has demonstrated this potential early on. title: The problem with polarization research in an age of extremism
authors: Felipe Vilanova; Flavio Azevedo
OSINT_Source_Types: Social Media; News Media; Government Data
Tags: political psychology; social polarization; democratic erosion; misinformation; anti-democratic movements
abstract: "I have been gravely disappointed with the white moderate. I have almost reached the regrettable conclusion that the Negro's great stumbling block in his stride toward freedom is not the White Citizen's Counciler or the Ku Klux Klanner, but the white moderate, who is more devoted to "order" than to justice; who prefers a negative peace which is the absence of tension to a positive peace which is the presence of justice"-Martin Luther King, Jr., Letter from Birmingham Jail (1963) Over the past decade, political polarization has become one of the most studied topics in the behavioral and social sciences. A search for "political polarization" in the Web of Science database (conducted on 23 October 2025) yielded 13,387 results, and more than 60 per cent of which were published since 2020. During the same period, authoritarian and anti-democratic movements have gained strength across diverse contexts. A Friedrich-Ebert-Stiftung (2023) study reported that support for a dictatorship led by a "strong leader" in Germany increased, and endorsement of social-Darwinist statements such as "some lives are worthless" more than doubled compared with previous years. Similarly, the Leipzig Authoritarianism Study (Decker et al., 2024) reported renewed increases in xenophobia, chauvinism, and the downplaying of Nazism. In Germany, national reports indicate that extremist offenses have increased year after year (Reuters, 2024). Similar patterns are also observable in the United States and Brazil where not only national surveys reveal an increased openness to military coups and one-party rule (Pew Research Center, 2024a;2024b) but also extremists have attempted to overturn electoral outcomes by storming their respective congresses (European Parliament, 2023).Authoritarian, conservative, and reactionary networks have been among the principal drivers of the surge in distrust, conspiracism, and anti-scientific sentiment (Backhaus et al., 2023;Benegal et al, 2022;Cologna et al., 2025;Dakin et al., 2023;Pavlovic et al., 2022;Philipp-Müller et al., 2022;van Bavel et al, 2022;van der Linden, 2020;Vlasceanu et al., 2024;Voelkel et al., 2024). Coordinated reactionary movements couple conspiratorial and antiscientific narratives as a central element of their political strategy, which, in the recent decades, has been successful in undermining pluralist opponents, galvanizing reactionary followers, shifting blame away from authoritarian sources, and weakening democratic values and institutions (Ren et al., 2022). Through national and international political campaigns, these networks have attempted to replace societal trust and factual knowledge with alternative belief systems that frame them both as victims of ideological attacks and as opponents of a corrupt establishment against the "will of the people" (Golder, 2016;Mudde, 2007;Rothmund et al., 2020;2022;Van Hauwaert et al, 2018;2020). By portraying democracy and science as corrupt, elitist, or ideologically captured establishments, these actors erode public trust in civic society, empirical inquiry, and in the institutions that sustain both (van der Meer et al., 2026). Moreover, in framing pluralist democracy and scientific expertise as nefarious, they reframe their quest for political power and dominance as a project of "renewal" and "social progress".foot_0 This rather paradoxical-and populist (Golder, 2016;Mudde, 2007)-framing has been a potent tool spreading and legitimizing hostility towards pluralist democracy and its knowledge-based institutions. Opposition to democracy, science, and institutional expertise is therefore not an accidental feature of anti-democratic populism and authoritarianism, but its defining component (Capraro et al., 2026;Levitsky and Ziblatt, 2019;Sandel, 2022;Snyder, 2021;Stanley, 2020).These developments illustrate that contemporary polarization does not occur in a vacuum. Rather, it is embedded within a wider ideological shift towards authoritarianism marked by the normalization of violence, systematic dis/misinformation campaigns, and hostility toward scientific expertise (Azevedo and Jost, 2021;Roozenbeek and van der Linden, 2024;van der Linden, 2023;Van Dijck et al., 2018;Vilanova et al., 2025). Against this backdrop, the widespread assumption of the field that polarization is inherently detrimental warrants re-examination. Notably, most empirical studies conceptualize polarization either in ideological terms-typically as increasing divergence or clustering of citizens' attitudes along an ideological spectrum (Philipp-Müller et al., 2022)-or in affective terms, defined as intensified animus toward political outgroups coupled with ingroup favoritism (Iyengar et al., 2012). While analytically tractable, this approach is nonetheless conceptually naïve and methodologically too coarse for the current political moment. Polarization can reflect (i) agonistic democratic conflict-a constitutive feature of pluralist democracy in which adversaries contest values and policies while accepting democratic norms, rules, and outcomes (Mouffe, 2000)-or (ii) democratically corrosive conflict, in which political identity is asymmetrically organized and strategically exploited into distrustful camps and wielded to justify rule-breaking, delegitimizing of elections and pluralist institutions, and erosion of accountability (Somer and McCoy, 2019). Rather than reflecting a symmetric breakdown of civility, this form of dissension constitutes a well-documented, elite-driven pathway to democratic backsliding (Bermeo, 2016). This framing constitutes a tacit belief that social harmony and dialogue are the ideal state of democratic life, and political antagonism is a failure of civility. In the current moment of rising extremism, not all polarization is equivalent nor undesirable. Historical experience underscores this asymmetry. Normative appeals to civility and harmony, when applied indiscriminately, can obscure the moral distinction between domination and resistance-condemning confrontational opposition even in contexts where democratic survival or basic human dignity is at stake. Under conditions of systematic persecution, conflict is not a pathology of democracy but one of the few remaining mechanisms through which injustice can be resisted. Moreover, opposition between democratic and authoritarian values cannot be treated as symmetrical disagreement. When one side rejects pluralism, electoral legitimacy, or epistemic accountability, then 'reconciliation' and even 'civility' function less as neutral civic virtues than as asymmetric constraints, and they risk unilaterally disarming prodemocracy forces. Indeed, democratically corrosive conflict primarily disciplines democratic defenders while leaving anti-democratic actors free to escalate, delegitimize, and capture institutions. Under such asymmetric conditions, appeals to reduce polarization risk equating resistance to authoritarianism with intolerance.The assumption that polarization must be reduced carries profound epistemic and political consequences for social and political psychology and political science. It shapes research designs, variable operationalizations, and the normative interpretations of findings. Interventions aimed at reducing affective polarization, for example, often promote empathy and perspective-taking across ideological divides without considering whether the underlying attitudes sustain or undermine the very conditions that allow for democratic discourse and evidence-based reasoning (e.g., Gillissen et al., 2025;Santos et al., 2022;Tausch et al., 2024;Voelkel et al., 2024;Warner et al., 2020). When authoritarian and anti-scientific movements are gaining strength, such naïve depolarization efforts risk demobilizing both scientific communities and democratic publics at precisely the moment when active defense of evidence and reasoned inquiry is most needed. Safeguarding the norms that make civil society and empirical inquiry possible requires an explicit commitment to democratic rules, pluralism, and epistemic standards (e.g., electoral legitimacy, institutional accountability, and evidence-constrained claims-making). We therefore propose a distinction between democratic polarization-agonistic contestation among actors who accept multicultural pluralism, democratic institutions and election outcomes, civil and human rights, and epistemic accountability-and antidemocratic polarization, in which conflict is strategically mobilized to delegitimize opponents, erode institutional constraints, and normalize dehumanization, scapegoating, misinformation, antiscientific, and conspiratorial narratives as a route to political power. To clarify this distinction, we introduce a minimal framework specifying when political conflict remains within democratic bounds and when it begins to undermine them. This clarification is necessary because much of the polarization literature treats all forms of conflict as analytically comparable, regardless of their implications for democratic functioning.Under contemporary conditions of authoritarian resurgence, disinformation, and institutional erosion, the meaning of democratic conflict cannot be inferred from intensity, incivility, or ideological distance alone. Research treating polarization as presumptively pathological may misclassify democratic resistance as equivalent to anti-democratic escalation. Indeed, democratic politics is not reducible to civility, consensus, or the absence of conflict. In pluralistic societies, conflict is often unavoidable and sometimes necessary. Political disagreement commonly concerns collective priorities, the allocation of public resources, and the design of institutions that structure social life. But democratic disagreement does not extend without limit. It unfolds within a democratic framework that must secure equal standing, basic rights, and meaningful inclusion for all members of the polity. Polarization researchers must contextualize and interpret political conflict in light of the political reality, including stakeholders' commitments to pluralism, equal citizenship, collective welfare, equitable social and economic opportunities, and the maintenance of democratic institutions.This point is especially important because the boundary between "ordinary" policy disagreement and anti-democratic politics cannot be drawn by separating social and economic arrangements from questions of rights, recognition, and membership. Social and economic arrangements are not external to democratic inclusion: they help determine who has security, health, mobility, voice, and genuine access to collective life. When such arrangements systematically expose some groups to deprivation, precarity, premature death, or political marginalization while insulating dominant groups and concentrated economic power, they cease to be ordinary policy disagreements in any normatively innocent sense. They become mechanisms through which hierarchy is organized and reproduced. For this reason, conflicts over rights, recognition, and membership cannot be cleanly separated from conflicts over economic life, since the latter shape whether democratic citizenship is substantive or merely formal. When such inequalities are re-described as routine political disagreement, domination is recast as policy and anti-democratic projects can pass as normal democratic contestation.Accordingly, not all politically salient disagreements are democratically equivalent. Some conflicts occur within democratic bounds, even when they are intense and adversarial. Others target the very conditions that make democratic life possible. When political actors seek to deny full standing to opponents, restrict who counts as a legitimate member of the polity, erode rights protections, weaponize institutions, or degrade the epistemic conditions of public reasoning, the issue is no longer conflict within democracy alone. It is conflict over democracy's own normative and institutional basis. Under such conditions, domination can be redescribed as policy, exclusion can be redescribed as disagreement, and anti-democratic projects can pass as routine democratic contestation.To tentatively point out this distinction, Table 1 provides a heuristic framework for evaluating when political conflict remains consistent with democratic functioning and when it does not. It is not intended as a rigid classificatory scheme, nor does it aim to resolve ambiguities inherent to sociopolitical realities. Rather, it identifies a set of dimensions along which political conflict may be evaluated. The purpose of Table 1 is to make explicit why polarization cannot be evaluated solely in terms of distance, hostility, or civility, as it is often the case in the published literature. What should matter most, in our view, is the nature and kind of conflict being mobilized, by whom, under what institutional conditions, and toward what ends. Contestation over collective priorities, public resources, and institutional arrangements within a framework that protects equal standing, basic rights, and democratic inclusion.Mobilization directed at domination, exclusion, scapegoating, or the denial of full standing, often while being framed as ordinary policy disagreement.The key distinction is not between "material" and "symbolic" conflict, but between conflict within democratic bounds and conflict targeting democracy's normative basic tenets. Treating social and economic arrangements as if they were innocent or neutral background can obscure the existence of democratically corrosive conflict.Opponents are treated as legitimate adversaries within a shared political community, even amid deep disagreement.Opponents are cast as enemies, traitors, contaminants, or groups whose equal standing should be denied.In unequal systems, claims for equal inclusion are often re-described as divisive or extreme.Commitment to fair representation, meaningful participation, and peaceful transfer of power under substantively democratic conditions.Fraud claims without evidence, voter suppression, intimidation, rule manipulation, or other efforts to entrench minority rule.Procedural compliance alone is insufficient. Elections can be formally lawful yet substantively distorted by gerrymandering, disenfranchisement, or unequal access.Rejection of intimidation, vigilantism, and coercive force as tools of routine political competition.Endorsement, normalization, or strategic toleration of violence, threats, or paramilitary intimidation to secure political ends.Defensive resistance under conditions of authoritarian aggression should not be treated as equivalent to aggression in the service of domination.Disagreement proceeds with some commitment to evidence, accountability, policy evaluation, and truth-seeking institutions, even when values differ.Strategic disinformation, conspiracy narratives, epistemic nihilism, anti-scientific attitudes, or the systematic rejection of evidence when it constrains preferred outcomes.Democratic politics requires agreement and commitment to epistemic infrastructure. Accountable institutions for truth-seeking and policy assessment are necessary while not devolving into technocracy nor preaching technocratic infallibility. Preserves equal citizenship, equal protection, minority rights, opposition rights, and inclusive participation as non-negotiable conditions of democratic life.Treats the rights, recognition, safety, or participation of some groups as contingent, revocable, or subordinate to dominantgroup interests.Formal rights may coexist with substantive exclusion. The issue is whether institutions protect equal standing just formally or de facto. A democracy that protects formal liberties while permitting social and economic arrangements that systematically hollow out equal membership remains democratic only in a thin and unstable sense.Supports and materially sustains the institutions that enable accountability, including independent courts, watchdogs, civil society, electoral administration, and a genuinely free press.Defunds, captures, delegitimizes, or selectively weaponizes accountability institutions in order to avoid scrutiny or entrench power.The performative acceptance of democracy is too weak a standard. Democratic conflict must occur while all actors help sustain democratic accountability infrastructures.Clarifies genuine social conflicts, widens participation, increases accountability, and surfaces injustices that require political resolution.Accelerates democratic erosion, normalizes exclusion, immunizes power from scrutiny, and recasts hierarchy as ordinary disagreement.Whether conflict is agonistic depends partly on background power relations, not merely on tone or intensity. For one example, civil and political liberties cannot be exercised meaningfully in the absence of the social and economic conditions that make equal membership, voice, and participation materially possible.This framework indicates that polarization cannot be adequately evaluated solely in terms of attitudinal distance, hostility, or affect. By distinguishing between agonistic and corrosive forms of conflict, it helps account for why comparable levels of polarization may have different implications across contexts. This distinction, however, introduces challenges for classification and measurement. Simpler models may offer greater clarity, parsimony, and cross-context comparability, but these advantages often involve the omission of distinctions that are normatively and politically relevant. As a result, analytically distinct forms of conflict may be treated as equivalent. title: Geochemical Insights into Rare Earth Elements Distribution patterns in Granites of Nasarawa State, North-Central Nigeria
authors: Mohammed Salihu; Emmanuel Joseph; Adamu N. Baba-Kutigi
Tags: Geological Analysis; Environmental Science; Mineral Resources; Nigeria
abstract: Purpose: To investigate the distribution of rare earth elements (REEs) in granites from selected locations within Nasarawa State, North-Central Nigeria, in order to evaluate their geochemical characteristics and assess their mineralization potential. Methodology: Fourteen (14) granite samples were systematically collected and analyzed using Instrumental Neutron Activation Analysis (INAA) at the Centre for Energy Research and Training, Ahmadu Bello University, Zaria, Nigeria. Trace element concentrations and REE patterns were examined, and geochemical discrimination diagrams were employed to determine tectonic settings and source characteristics. Findings: The trace element data show elevated concentrations of U (6.79 ppm), Co (64.81 ppm), Rb (266.89 ppm), Sb (2.04 ppm), Cs (43.83 ppm), and Ta (219.90 ppm) relative to Upper Continental Crust values, indicating moderate to extreme enrichment. REE patterns are dominated by light rare earth elements (LREE; average 58.44 ppm) over heavy rare earth elements (HREE; average 18.22 ppm). Geochemical discrimination diagrams indicate that the granites were emplaced in multiple tectonic settings, including syn-collisional, volcanic arc, and within-plate environments, and were primarily derived from the upper continental crust. Comparative analysis shows general depletion of most REEs, except for dysprosium (Dy), which exhibits elevated concentrations, suggesting potential Dy mineralization. Unique Contribution to Theory, Practice and Policy: This study provides baseline geochemical data for REE distribution in granites of Nasarawa State and contributes to the understanding of crustal evolution and tectonic settings in the region. It highlights the potential for dysprosium mineralization, offering practical insights for targeted REE exploration and resource development. The findings can inform mineral exploration strategies, guide investment decisions, and support policy formulation aimed at sustainable exploitation of REE resources in Nigeria.
Keywords: Rare Earth Elements; Geochemical Distribution; Granites; Nasarawa State; Mineralization Potential
affiliations: Niger State Polytechnic; Federal University Dutsin-Ma

title: A Unified Compliance Aggregator Framework for Automated Multi-Tool Security Assessment of Linux Systems
authors: Sheldon Paul; Izzat Alsmadi
Tags: security; vulnerability; assessment framework; Linux; compliance tools
abstract: Assessing the security posture of modern computing systems typically requires the use of multiple specialized tools. These tools focus on different aspects such as configuration compliance, file integrity, and vulnerability exposure, and their outputs are often difficult to interpret collectively. This paper introduces the Unified Compliance Aggregator (UCA), a framework that integrates several open-source security tools into a single composite score representing overall system security. The proposed framework combines outputs from Lynis, OpenSCAP (STIG and CIS profiles), AIDE, Tripwire, and Nmap NSE. A normalization process converts heterogeneous outputs into a consistent 0 to 100 scale, followed by weighted aggregation. We also introduce a logarithmic scoring model for file integrity measurements to address limitations observed in prior linear approaches. Experiments were conducted on Ubuntu 22.04 across different hardening levels and environments. Results show consistent improvement in composite scores as systems are hardened, while also revealing contrasting behavior between compliance and file integrity tools. Two case studies, a basic web server and a DVWA-based system illustrate how the framework can be applied in practical scenarios.
Keywords: security assessment; Linux systems; compliance; aggregation framework; open-source tools

title: Ethical AI in Cybersecurity
authors: Shritin Shetty; Md Mahmudul Hasan
venue: Advances in Computational Intelligence and Robotics
Tags: AI Ethics; Cybersecurity Frameworks; Transparency; Accountability
abstract: The integration of artificial intelligence into cybersecurity systems introduces significant ethical challenges that require systematic and measurable evaluation. Although prior studies propose lifecycle-based ethical AI frameworks, practical methodologies for benchmarking ethical compliance remain limited. This study presents an evidence-driven analytical framework for evaluating ethical integration in AI-driven cybersecurity research. A corpus of peer-reviewed publications was analyzed using a structured pipeline that employs large language models (LLMs) to extract machine-readable JSON evidence, followed by automated scoring across seven ethical dimensions: transparency, explainability, accountability, human oversight, privacy, data protection, and continuous ethical monitoring. The results reveal substantial variability in ethical compliance, with notable deficiencies in human oversight and post-deployment monitoring. The proposed framework is replicable, auditable, and supports evidence-based ethical governance of AI-enabled cybersecurity platforms.
Keywords: Ethical AI; Cybersecurity; Benchmarking; Compliance; Large Language Models

title: Disclosure of Cybersecurity Investments and the Cost of Capital
authors: Taha Havakhor; Mohammad S. Rahman; Tianjian Zhang
Tags: financial disclosure; corporate governance; risk management; capital markets
abstract: Executives often hesitate to disclose their company’s cybersecurity investments, fearing lawsuits or negative reactions from investors. Our research shows that transparency in this area actually pays off. Analyzing SEC filings from nearly 2,000 public firms, we find that companies that disclose cybersecurity investments enjoy a lower cost of capital—cheaper access to debt and equity financing. These benefits are strongest when disclosures are specific rather than boilerplate, when the firm is followed by more analysts and more institutional investors, and when disclosed investments in cybersecurity are substantial. The takeaway is clear: Meaningful disclosure builds trust with investors, who reward transparency by lowering financing costs. For leaders and regulators alike, this finding highlights that openness about cybersecurity readiness is not just good governance—It is smart business in a capital market that increasingly values risk management and resilience.
Keywords: cybersecurity investments; cost of capital; transparency; SEC filings; investor trust
affiliations: McGill University; Purdue University West Lafayette; California State University, Dominguez Hills

title: Awesome Cybersecurity
authors: Brandon Himpfen
venue: Zenodo (CERN European Organization for Nuclear Research)
Tags: trustworthy; repository; implementation
abstract: Initial release. The goal of this list is to: Aggregate trustworthy and relevant resources. Support learning, research, and practical implementation. Provide a centralized reference for practitioners, researchers, and developers. This release establishes the baseline version for citation, versioning, and long-term maintenance. Licensed under CC0 1.0 Universal.
