title: Optimization-Driven Multi-Domain Hybrid Framework for Phishing Detection Across URL, Network, and Email Layers Using Deep Learning and Heuristic Intelligence
authors: ASHVINI JADHAV; pankaj chandre
venue: Springer Science and Business Media LLC
Tags: Phishing; Deep Learning; Hybrid Systems; Optimization; Adversarial Conditions
abstract: Abstract Phishing attacks have evolved into click activity that operates across URL, network, and email channels simultaneously, automatic redirecting user, rendering single-domain detection systems insufficient data for real-world deployment. This research presents an optimization-driven multilayer multi-domain hybrid phishing detection framework that incorporates transport-layer TLS communication analysis, URL essential features, and email header, content features within an OSI-aligned three-cluster architecture (C0–Transport, C1–Session, C2–Application). Four available datasets totaling over 762,000 samples are employed, spanning URL lexical features, TLS infrastructure fingerprints, and hybrid multi-domain representations. A feature optimization pipeline based on the Heuristic-Constrained Class-Conditional Feature Selection (HC²FS) algorithm reduces dimensionality from 700 + features into three clusters by 25–50% per cluster while preserving discriminative power. Three deep learning architectures, Transformer, Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM), and Recurrent Neural Network (RNN) are comparatively under controlled adversarial feature-degradation scenarios simulating 20%, 30%, and 50% missing features. Experimental results demonstrate that LSTM achieves the highest overall performance, reaching 99.62% accuracy and AUC = 0.9949 on the TLS infrastructure dataset, at the same time Hybrid architecture provides the most robust performance under adversarial conditions, which reduces the gap in real word deployment. The proposed framework addresses six documented research gaps: dataset fragmentation, inconsistent feature engineering, suboptimal hyperparameter tuning, architectural isolation of detection components, interpretability deficit, and evaluation bias. The Modified Bonobo Optimizer (MBO) is applied for joint hyperparameter co-optimization, achieving F1 > 0.97 and false-positive rate (FPR)
Keywords: Phishing Detection; Deep Learning; Hybrid Framework; Feature Optimization; Network Security
affiliations: MIT Art, Design and Technology University

title: Exploring Cybersecurity Awareness Among Street Vendors: A Pathway to Inclusive Growth
authors: Apurva A. Chauhan; Vinita Choudhary
venue: Emerging Trends in Smart Computing and Sustainable Communication
OSINT_Source_Types: Public Records; Social Media; News Media
Tags: Cybersecurity; Digital Literacy; Online Safety; Small Business; India
abstract: Digitalization has globally transformed the business and offered exceptional opportunities for promoting inclusive growth. In today's digital world, cybersecurity has become a crucial issue for not only large companies but also for small business owners, such as street vendors. The Government of India also aims to make vendors a part of the formal economy by launching Digital India and PM SVANidhi Yojana. Street vendors, who form a significant part of the economy, are also increasingly adopting digital tools, especially mobile payments, to expand their businesses. The transformation to a digitalized society is also bringing cybersecurity threats for the stakeholders. A substantial gap lies between awareness of digital options and understanding the cybersecurity threats. Present research aims to explore the cybersecurity awareness among street vendors, focusing on how they interact with digital tools and the various vulnerabilities they face. The study is descriptive in nature, where data have been collected from Street vendors with the help of a questionnaire. The study reveals that street vendors are facing various cybersecurity threats, such as fake payment screenshots, suspicious links, and unauthorized deductions. Findings reveal that there is a significant impact of education on vulnerabilities to cyber threats. Many street vendors are not aware of how to protect themselves from online fraud and identify scams. The study suggests that policymakers, local government, NGOs, and various educational institutions should ensure a digital literacy framework. It is essential to promote cyber safety education among the informal sector so that all groups can benefit from the opportunities created by digitalization.
Keywords: Cybersecurity Awareness; Street Vendors; Digitalization; Cyber Threats; Economic Inclusion

title: Implications of artificial intelligence and cyberspace on risk management capabilities
authors: Hatidža Beriša; Vanja Cvetković; Aleksandar Pavić
venue: International Journal of Disaster Risk Management
Tags: AI in cybersecurity; risk management; modern warfare; information technology
abstract: The crisis management system is a key element of a modern, reliable, effective, and sustainable security system. In contemporary settings, management functions are implemented through the capabilities of a command-information system. Consequently, most activities are conducted within cyberspace, meaning that management functions cannot be effectively executed without adequate telecommunications and information technology support. The primary target of hostile cyber activities is precisely the command-information system. Developing an optimal methodology for the use of command-information systems, as well as for prevention and protection against cyber-attacks, is among the most critical tasks for modern defence and security systems. One approach to enhancing risk management is through the application of artificial intelligence (AI). AI should ensure superiority in cyberspace, primarily at the national level, providing the foundation for executing legitimate and lawful cyber activities in a state's defence system. This paper aims to explore the role and significance of artificial intelligence within the realm of cybersecurity by analyzing its implications for risk management in contemporary environments. Command in modern military operations, the nature of modern warfare risks, the protection of command-information systems, the autonomy of artificial intelligence in decision-making, and an understanding of cyber threats are factors through which the challenges of modern command systems can be understood.
Keywords: artificial intelligence; cybersecurity; risk management; command-information systems; cyber threats

title: AI and Digital Evidence in Indian Sexual Assault Cases: A Legal-Ethical Analysis
authors: Hamda Akhtarul Arfeen
Tags: Artificial Intelligence; Digital Evidence; Forensic Science; Justice System; Legal Framework
abstract: This research examines the integration of artificial intelligence in India's digital forensic landscape, with particular emphasis on its application in sexual assault inquiries. As India confronts escalating cybercrime rates and increasingly complex digital evidence, forensic laboratories in the country have begun implementing artificial intelligence driven solutions to process and analyse electronic data. The study investigates specific artificial intelligence tools deployed by agencies such as the Central Forensic Science Laboratory and state police cyber cells, including facial recognition systems, automated malware analysis tools, among others. The paper encompasses this analysis in the instances of sexual assault forensics, where substantial backlogs and manual processing methods contribute to delayed justice. It discovers how artificial intelligence models, including automated biological evidence analysis, accelerated DNA matching, intelligent evidence prioritisation systems, and unconventional digital evidence processing, can potentially reduce evidence dispensation time from months to days, enabling faster filing of chargesheets within statutory limits. The research further examines how these technologies can be integrated with existing initiatives such as the National Cyber Crime Reporting Portal and the Indian Cyber Crime Coordination Centre (I4C) to create specialised pathways for rape and child abuse cases. Ethical and legal concerns are a major aspect when the usage of AI in forensics is put forward. The paper will sketch how the new Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam, 2023, deals with this amalgamation of AI and its interaction with India's constitutional framework, the new Digital Personal Data Protection Act (DPDP), and the existing IT Act. Additionally, it analyses recent judicial attitudes toward digital evidence and also compares the usage of AI forensic tools in different countries and how the usage has helped the police and judicial authorise to speed up the justice process. The research concludes by proposing a comprehensive ethical framework tailored to guide the responsible integration of AI in India's digital forensic ecosystem, balancing the benefits of AI, including drastically reduced evidence processing times, heightened detection competencies, and expedited judicial proceedings, with essential protections to algorithmic transparency standards and vigorous data protection safeguards. By potentially reducing investigation timelines from years to months, AI integration offers transformative potential for delivering timely justice to sexual assault survivors, which can resolve key loopholes in criminal investigations.
Keywords: AI; Digital Forensics; Sexual Assault; India; Legal-Ethical Analysis; Cybercrime; Evidence Processing
